---
title = "Basics"
---

This page reviews some of the basics of Quarto and Markdown syntax supported by *Calepin*. It covers document structure, code chunks, figures, math, cross-references, citations, and other features that work out of the box.

# Getting started

*Calepin* renders `.qmd` documents to HTML, LaTeX, Typst, and Markdown. It executes R code chunks, captures output and plots, resolves citations and cross-references, and wraps everything in a template.

To render this tutorial:

```
calepin tutorial.qmd
```

# Markdown basics

*Calepin* supports standard CommonMark and GitHub Flavored Markdown. The table below summarizes the basic Markdown notation from the original specification.

| Element | Syntax |
|---|---|
| Heading | `# H1`, `## H2`, ..., `###### H6` |
| Paragraph | Blank line between blocks of text |
| Line break | Two trailing spaces or `\` at end of line |
| Italic | `*text*` or `_text_` |
| Bold | `**text**` or `__text__` |
| Bold + italic | `***text***` |
| Blockquote | `> quoted text` |
| Ordered list | `1. item` |
| Unordered list | `- item`, `* item`, or `+ item` |
| Inline code | `` `code` `` |
| Code block | Indent by 4 spaces or fence with ` ``` ` |
| Horizontal rule | `---`, `***`, or `___` |
| Link | `[text](url)` or `[text](url "title")` |
| Reference link | `[text][id]` then `[id]: url` |
| Image | `![alt text](path)` |
| Escape | `\*`, `\[`, `\\`, etc. |

Here is a paragraph about Montreal that illustrates all of these features.

{% include "source_tip" %}

---

## Montréal

> *Montréal is a city of contrasts: old-world charm meets modern ambition.*

Montréal is the largest city in the province of Québec and the *second most populous* in Canada. Founded in 1642, the city sits on an island in the *Saint Lawrence River*. Visitors should explore these landmarks:

1. Old Montréal: cobblestone streets and the [Notre-Dame Basilica](https://www.basiliquenotredame.ca)
2. Mount Royal: the park designed by Frederick Law Olmsted
3. The Plateau: colourful row houses and lively cafés

Local specialties worth trying:

- Poutine
- Smoked meat sandwiches
- Bagels from [St-Viateur](https://www.stviateurbagel.com "St-Viateur Bagel")

The city's motto is `Concordia Salus`[^mtl], meaning "well-being through harmony."

[^mtl]: The motto was adopted in 1833 and appears on the city's coat of arms.

As the Montréal tourism board puts it:\
"*There is no place quite like it.*"

![A view of the Montréal skyline](/assets/placeholder.png)


# Code chunks

Code chunks are fenced with triple backticks and a `{r}` header. Options go inside the chunk as `#|` pipe comments. See the [Chunks](/code/chunks.html) vignette for the full list of chunk options.

```{r}
#| echo = fenced
x <- 1:10
mean(x)
```


# Inline code

R expressions can be evaluated inline using back ticks and curly braces. See the [Inline](/code/inline.html) vignette for more details.

```
`{r} round(pi, 4)`
```

The numbers in this sentence are computed dynamically: The mean of 1:10 is `{r} mean(1:10)` and pi is approximately `{r} round(pi, 4)`.


# Figures

```{r, scatter}
#| fig-width = 5
#| fig-height = 4
#| fig-cap = "A simple scatter plot"
plot(
    1:10,
    (1:10)^2,
    main = "Square Numbers",
    xlab = "x",
    ylab = "x^2"
)
```


# Cross-references

*Calepin* resolves references to figures, sections, and theorem environments.

* @fig-scatter renders as a linked reference
* @sec-figures links to the Figures section
* [-@fig-scatter] suppresses the type prefix

Sections get automatic ids from their heading text.


# Math

Inline math: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$. Display math:

$$
\int_0^1 x^2 \, dx = \frac{1}{3}
$$

::: {.theorem #thm-pythagoras}
In a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Let a right triangle have legs $a$, $b$ and hypotenuse $c$. By constructing squares on each side and comparing areas, we obtain $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.
:::

By @thm-pythagoras, the relationship is fundamental to Euclidean geometry.


# Callouts

Callout divs highlight important information. Five types are available: `callout-note`, `callout-warning`, `callout-tip`, `callout-caution`, and `callout-important`. They can be nested.

:::: {.callout-note}
This is a note callout. Use it for supplementary information.

::: {.callout-warning}
Callouts can be nested. This warning appears inside the note above.
:::
::::


# Conditional content

Fenced divs with `.content-visible` or `.content-hidden` classes control which content appears in each output format.

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
This paragraph only appears in HTML output.
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-format="latex"}
This paragraph is hidden in LaTeX but visible in all other formats.
:::

Format aliases are supported: `latex` and `pdf` both match LaTeX output, `markdown` and `md` both match Markdown.


# Raw blocks

Raw blocks inject format-specific markup that passes through verbatim when the output format matches and is dropped otherwise.

```{=html}
<details><summary>Click to expand</summary>
<p>This raw HTML only appears in HTML output.</p>
</details>
```


# Tabsets

Tabsets organize content into switchable tabs. In HTML, readers click to switch tabs. In LaTeX and Typst, tabs render as regular sections.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R

```{r}
x <- c(1, 2, 3)
mean(x)
```

## Python

```python
x = [1, 2, 3]
sum(x) / len(x)
```

## Julia

```julia
x = [1, 2, 3]
sum(x) / length(x)
```

:::

Tabsets with the same `group` attribute switch together:

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## R

R version of example 2.

## Python

Python version of example 2.

:::

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## R

R content stays synced with the tabset above.

## Python

Python content stays synced too.

:::


# Line blocks

Line blocks preserve line breaks and leading spaces. Prefix each line with `|`.

| The limerick packs laughs anatomical
| Into space that is quite economical.
|    But the good ones I've seen
|    So seldom are clean
| And the clean ones so seldom are comical.


# Footnotes

There are two ways to create footnotes. Named references use `[^label]` at the point of reference and `[^label]: text` for the definition elsewhere in the document. Inline footnotes use `^[text]` directly without a separate definition.

Named footnote[^3] and inline footnote^[This is defined right here.].

[^3]: This is defined separately.


# Numbered sections

Add `number-sections: true` to the YAML front matter to automatically number all section headings.


# The `.hidden` div

The `.hidden` div executes its content but produces no output.

::: {.hidden}
```{r}
secret <- 42
```
:::

The value computed silently: `{r} secret`.
